game_name: Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels

danger_signals.visual_form: Mushroom sprite with distinct visual variant from power-up mushroom
danger_signals.meaning: Anti-power-up that shrinks or kills player; acts as trap disguised as benefit
Mobile hazard; collision causes damage unless player jumps on it

danger_signals.visual_form: Empty space below platforms; black void
danger_signals.meaning: Instant death hazard; unavoidable fall results in life loss

danger_signals.visual_form: No visual representation; manifests only through collision
danger_signals.meaning: Hidden platform danger; player must memorize or discover placement through trial

danger_signals.visual_form: Physics manifestation; player redirected during jump arc
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard that disrupts trajectory and causes missed jumps

danger_signals.visual_form: Large enemy sprite with distinctive crown and fire-breathing animation
danger_signals.meaning: Final-stage antagonist; boss encounter requiring pattern recognition and precision

safety_signals.meaning: Increases player size and survivability; grants extra hit point
Grants offensive capability; allows ranged attack against enemies
Temporary invulnerability; all enemies become harmless and collectible

safety_signals.visual_form: Brick or stone tile blocks with defined edges
safety_signals.meaning: Safe ground; collision detection allows standing and jumping
Level progression shortcut; allows skipping to later worlds (but may lock alternate paths)
Secret passage; leads to hidden areas and bonus content

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: M logo on chest (Mario); character silhouette distinct from Luigi
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player sprite size changes when powered up; visual feedback through animation states

faction_indicators.color_identity: Dark/Brown (Bowser); varied enemy coloration (Red Koopa Troopa, etc.)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Bowser crown and fire-breathing; turtle shell imagery for Koopa enemies
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Red Koopa (mobile ground threat); other enemy types with distinct sprites
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle architecture in boss stages; enemy clustering in later worlds signals faction presence
Castle throne room at world endings; Toad NPC at stage completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Simpler level layouts with wider platforms; more predictable enemy placement; clearer visual distinction between safe and dangerous areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies follow simple patrol patterns; fewer simultaneous hazards; forgiving jump windows

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased visual density; more complex platform configurations; poison mushroom introduction; narrower safety margins in level design
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy variety increases; wind gusts and invisible blocks introduce; warp zones with negative consequences appear

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Extreme visual complexity; minimal safe space; heavy use of visual tricks (poison mushrooms indistinguishable from power-ups); dense enemy clustering
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Requires frame-perfect inputs; invisible blocks become essential mechanic; counterproductive warps force specific routing

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Isolated arena; Bowser sprite dominates screen; fire attack visual effects; throne room castle interior
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Bowser follows attack pattern; projectile avoidance required; no escape; pattern-based vulnerability window

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stars appear on title screen after each 8-4 completion; world select menu visual feedback; achievement of hidden worlds
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: New worlds unlock; difficulty scaling continues; rewards skill-based progression

environmental_storytelling.example: Boss stages feature castle interiors; final world 8-4 emphasizes throne room setting; visual narrative of approaching Bowser's domain
Visual complexity and platform density increase per world; early worlds use repeating simple patterns; later worlds show visual chaos communicating danger
Toad's 'Princess is in another castle' statement after boss defeats; narrative progression signal through dialogue rather than environmental visuals
Poison mushrooms absent from early worlds; introduced mid-game as visual trick mechanic; signals rising difficulty through new threat category
Marketing text on FDS cartridge packaging communicates design intent; meta-level signal that difficulty has intentionally escalated beyond original game

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Fast acceleration/deceleration; lower jump arc; standard running speed
Larger sprite size; survives one hit before shrinking; increased visual profile
Throws fireballs on input; can destroy enemies at range; animation change during fire throw
Sprite flashes with invulnerability animation; enemies become collectible; all hazards harmless
Slower acceleration/deceleration; higher jump arc; designed for skilled technical play
Walks platform patrol route; reverses direction at edges; predictable movement pattern
Enters shell state; can be kicked as projectile; slides across platforms when kicked
Shoots fireballs in attack pattern; jumps to traverse arena; vulnerable during specific animation windows
Static in block; visually indistinguishable from power-up mushroom until close inspection
Shrinks player or causes death; acts as trap; causes immediate negative state change

notes: Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels primarily communicates world-state through level design density and visual complexity rather than explicit narrative. Danger is signaled through platform scarcity, enemy clustering, and introduction of counter-intuitive hazards (poison mushrooms, backwards warps). No political factions exist; instead, a simple hero-vs-antagonist binary (Mario vs. Bowser) is communicated through sprite design and arena architecture. Character differentiation (Mario vs. Luigi) is behavioral rather than visual. The game's difficulty progression serves as primary world-state indicator: visual simplicity early = tutorial; visual chaos late = expertise required. Invisible blocks and poison mushroom ambiguity represent game's core design philosophy: trust and mastery through failure.
